title: Choose Your Own Adventure - House of Danger
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8fwEHUkKTFc

Daniel (@dungeondive) revisits a wave of childhood nostalgia with Choose Your Own Adventure: House of Danger, Prospero Hall and Z-Man's board-and-card adaptation of R.A. Montgomery's psychic-detective gamebook. He's candid about why it suits him right now: on a complexity scale of one to ten it's "probably a zero" — a small-box game you set up and play in minutes, exactly the low-stakes comfort his current headspace wants.

You work through five chapters, each with a goal, by reading story cards (text on the front, art and a choice or d6 challenge on the back, tested against a rising Danger Meter). A parallel deck of clue cards supplies items, stat boosts and extra story branches, and a psychic-scale track and a "premonition" image you study beforehand add a clever divination layer. He praises how well it ports the genre to the table — better integrated than Story Master's Tales — with maybe two or three playthroughs of replay before you've seen every clue, and no real fail state (lose psychic points, then retry the other choice). His one genuinely funny complaint: the cards are inexplicably, table-coatingly dusty.

A warm recommendation for a relaxing co-op night with a partner or a non-gamer, and a fond tribute to the books that hooked a generation.

----

Which Choose Your Own Adventure or gamebook from childhood hooked you on the hobby — and does a tabletop adaptation capture that magic for you?
